turns.text: Hello Camellia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s… it’s a lot to put into words. Honestly, just sitting here, I’m hyper-aware of every little pull in my abdomen. At forty-three, you don’t get to be blissfully ignorant. I’ve spent the last nine months reading every paper I could find on placental insufficiency and tracking fetal movement with a focus that probably borders on obsessive. But it’s not because I want to be a "geriatric" medical case study; it’s because I’ve lost a baby before. That ghost is always sitting right there in the room with me.

The medical side, the stats, the risks—I know them all. But the part no one seems to write about in the textbooks is the sheer logistics of trying to bring a life into the world when you don't even own a car. Every time I have a Braxton Hicks contraction, I don't just think about the labor; I start calculating the cost and the reliability of a ride-share. If I go into active labor at 3:00 AM and an app decides to surge-price or cancel, what do I do? That panic is constant. I see these younger women at the clinic with their moms and sisters holding their hands, and I just feel… isolated. It’s just me and my husband, navigating this alone. I just need someone to see that I’m more than my age.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I’ll be honest—I spent weeks on that birth plan. I had it typed up, color-coded, and printed out in a neat little folder. I wanted to be that patient who was "prepared." Because of my age and the history of loss, I thought having it all written down would make me feel in control. I thought the staff would look at it and see a woman who was educated, someone who had done her homework on every intervention and threshold. 

I expected them to treat it like a map. I thought, "If I have this, they’ll respect my wishes, they’ll see me." But when things started getting real—when the contractions became these waves I couldn't breathe through—the plan felt like a scrap of paper. The medical team was polite, sure, but I felt like they were constantly looking at me through the lens of my age, not my preferences. They’d nod at my requests and then pivot back to their checklists.

When I was in the thick of it, the plan just… vanished. It became irrelevant because I couldn't focus on anything but the fear that I wouldn't make it through safely, or that I’d missed something critical. It wasn't the collaborative experience I’d hoped for. It felt like I was reciting lines from a play no one else was watching.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

It happened when the monitoring started showing some deceleration. I had been so adamant about wanting to move around—I’d read all the studies on how upright positioning helps with fetal descent—but the moment they saw those dips on the monitor, the room’s atmosphere just shifted. 

The calm, professional demeanor of the nurses sharpened into something much more clinical. They didn't really ask; they just started moving things, attaching wires, and telling me I needed to stay flat on the bed for "continuous monitoring." I tried to argue, to explain that I understood the risks of the decelerations but still wanted to be mobile, but my voice just sounded thin. I felt like I was being silenced. They weren't seeing a person who had spent months studying this; they were just seeing an "at-risk" pregnancy that needed to be managed, efficiently and by the book. 

I remember feeling this wave of helplessness, not because I didn't trust them to keep the baby safe, but because I felt my autonomy being stripped away in real-time. I had spent so much energy trying to be the informed partner in my care, but in that moment, I realized my role wasn't to lead—it was just to comply. It was humiliating, really, to be reduced to a set of vitals.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I’d read everything—the pros and cons of nitrous, the risks of epidurals, the way position changes can mitigate the intensity. I expected a conversation, a real dialogue where my midwives would help me navigate the pain based on how I was coping in the moment. I thought there would be this steady, supportive guidance where my preferences were weighed against the clinical reality, almost like a partnership.

But the reality? It felt like the options were treated as a binary: either you’re "trying" to do it unmedicated, or you’re ready for the epidural. When the intensity finally hit that point where I was gasping, the offer for the epidural felt less like a choice and more like a default. It wasn’t "How are you holding up, Camellia?" It was, "You look like you’re struggling; let’s get the anesthesiologist." 

I felt this pressure to stay "strong," to prove I could handle it, which I think was my way of fighting back against the "geriatric" label they’d slapped on me. I wanted to prove I wasn't fragile. But when I finally did accept the medication, I felt a crushing sense of failure. Nobody told me it was okay to change my mind, or that my pain wasn't a sign of weakness. I just felt like I’d finally "broken" under the pressure of the room.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I really wanted to try hydrotherapy—the birthing tub. I had read so much about how the warm water helps take the edge off and allows you to move your body in ways that gravity doesn't permit. It felt like the one thing that could actually help me feel human while everything else was being dictated by monitors and clinical protocols.

But as soon as the medical team started getting concerned about those decelerations I mentioned, the tub was off the table instantly. There wasn't a discussion about whether we could monitor me in the water or if there were alternatives. It was just a hard "no." I felt like because I was considered "medium-risk" due to my age, they were extra cautious, which meant any tool that might have made me feel more comfortable or in control was immediately flagged as a liability. 

It felt like they were managing a piece of machinery that might break, not a woman in labor. I wasn't just denied a tub; I was denied the chance to have a dignified, slightly more peaceful experience. I understand they’re protecting the baby, I really do, but that doesn't mean it didn't hurt to have my preferences discarded so coldly. It made me feel like my comfort was completely secondary to their need to mitigate any possible risk.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

When we finally walked through those hospital doors, my stomach was in knots. I’d spent months imagining a warm, almost ritualistic space where people would acknowledge that this was my first—and likely only—chance at this. I wanted to be welcomed as a person, not a chart number. I kept hoping for someone to look me in the eye and say, "We’re here with you, you're doing great."

Instead, it felt like checking into an airport terminal that was already behind schedule. It was cold, bright, and hurried. Everyone was focused on the screens and the paperwork. There was no acknowledgment of the specific anxiety I was carrying, or the distance we’d had to travel just to get there. I felt small and exposed, just another older woman in a drafty gown, shuffling down a hallway that smelled of antiseptic and exhaustion. 

The environment was sterile, which I know is "safe" in a medical sense, but it felt incredibly isolating. My husband tried to hold my hand, but there was nowhere for us to really exist as a couple—we were just two people squeezed into a corner of a room dominated by equipment. It didn't feel like a sanctuary for birth; it felt like a site of management. It was jarring, and it made me retreat into myself even more.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise—and the most jarring one—was how quickly I was stripped of my personhood the moment I stepped into that room. I had built up this mental image of a "team" approach, thinking the nurses and I would be working toward the same goal. But the room felt like it belonged to the machines, not to me. 

There was this constant, rhythmic beeping from the monitors, and the lighting was this harsh, clinical fluorescent buzz that made everything feel exposed and frantic. I remember looking at the bed and feeling like it wasn't a place for a mother to birth her child, but a piece of furniture meant for a procedure. 

What shocked me most was that nobody asked me what *I* needed to feel settled. Not once did anyone say, "What would make this space feel better for you?" The atmosphere was entirely dictated by the hospital’s pace, not the pace of my labor. It was as if my presence was an intrusion on their routine rather than the reason they were there. I expected to feel like a participant in my own birth, but the physical environment made it clear—I was just the raw material they were tasked with processing. It was a cold, lonely realization.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

Because I had spent so much time reading, I truly believed that if an intervention became necessary, we would have a moment—a pause—where the provider would explain the "why," the risks, the benefits, and the alternatives. I expected a collaborative conversation, even if it had to be brief. I wanted to be treated like an intelligent partner in my own medical care.

But when things started to slide away from the "plan," that idea of shared decision-making evaporated instantly. When they decided I needed an augmentation, it wasn't a discussion. It was a series of quick, clipped sentences spoken *at* me, not *to* me. They were already opening packages and hooking up lines while the words were still leaving their mouths. It felt like they were informing me of a decision that had already been made in the hallway, or perhaps just decided on by habit.

There was no space for me to ask questions. Every time I tried to interject, I felt like I was being "difficult" or "anxious." I wasn't being difficult; I was trying to keep my head above water in a room that felt like it was spinning out of my control. I realized then that my "education" didn't matter. To them, my voice was just noise in a high-stakes, time-sensitive situation.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I suppose I had this romanticized notion of a midwife as someone who would act as a kind of anchor. I expected them to be the person who could look past the monitor, recognize the "ghost" of my previous loss, and hold space for that. I wanted someone to coach me, to remind me that I was a mother, not just a forty-three-year-old pregnancy at risk for complications. I wanted that human, empathetic connection—someone to say, "I see how hard you’re working," or even just to offer a reassuring touch that wasn't checking my dilation.

But the reality was a revolving door of staff. The clinical tasks—the blood pressure cuffs, the internal exams, the charting—took up all the oxygen in the room. If a nurse was in the room, her eyes were almost always on the screen, not me. When they spoke, it was always to report a status or a directive. 

It felt like I was a project they were managing between shifts. There was no personal attention because they were simply too rushed, too focused on the "medium-risk" label to see the person behind it. I didn't feel supported; I felt processed. I had so much emotional weight, so much fear, and it had nowhere to go because nobody asked. I was essentially alone in a room full of people.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

There was a moment, somewhere in the middle of it all, when the shift change happened. The nurse who had been there for most of the morning—the one who only spoke in bullet points—handed over the chart to a new nurse. I remember the new nurse walked in, stopped, and actually paused. She looked at me, not the monitor, and she noticed I was shivering. It wasn't from the cold, it was from the sheer adrenaline and the terror I’d been holding in for hours.

She didn’t reach for a blood pressure cuff. Instead, she reached for a warm blanket, tucked it around my shoulders, and just rested her hand on my arm for a second. She didn’t say anything medical. She just whispered, "I know you're doing the hardest work there is." 

That was it. That was the moment I felt human again. It was so small, but it felt like a lifeline. But then, as quickly as that moment arrived, the monitor started chirping again, and she was pulled back into the clinical flow. It was like a brief flickering light in a very dark room. The contrast only made the rest of the time—when I felt invisible and just like a "geriatric" statistic—feel even sharper and lonelier. I was a person for a heartbeat, and then I was just a patient again.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had this vision of the "Golden Hour." I’d read all about the importance of immediate skin-to-skin contact for bonding and regulation. I imagined the room going quiet, the lights dimmed, and someone—the midwife, maybe—placing my baby on my chest so we could just breathe together. I thought my husband would be right there, and we would finally just be a family. It was the only thing that kept me going through the fear.

But the reality was chaotic. When the baby arrived, there was no gentle transition. There was a flurry of activity, voices calling out vitals, and a rush of pediatric staff. Because of my age and the way the labor had ended, they seemed hyper-vigilant. My baby was whisked away to a warmer almost immediately for "observation." 

I lay there on that bed, feeling empty and raw, watching them work on my child from a distance. My husband was hovering between me and the warmer, looking just as panicked as I felt. I didn't get to hold them until what felt like an eternity later. That vital, peaceful hour I’d banked so much hope on? It was just a blur of clinical intervention. I didn't get to welcome my baby; I just got to watch them be processed by the hospital. It was heartbreaking.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

What stays with me is the silence. Not the kind of quiet that follows a storm, but a heavy, empty silence in the room while they worked on the baby a few feet away. I was still stitched up, shaking from the medication and the exhaustion, and I could hear the instruments clinking and the hum of the warmer, but I couldn't hear my baby’s rhythm against my chest.

I kept waiting for them to bring my child over. I expected someone to say, "Look, here’s your baby, they’re perfect." But there was no celebration, no "congratulations" that felt earned. There was just this intense focus on the numbers. I remember looking at my husband’s face—he was leaning over the warmer, looking like he was trying to memorize every detail of our baby because he was the only one allowed close—and I felt like I was excluded from the most important introduction of my life.

I still wake up at night feeling that weight of being separated from my own child. It wasn’t a medical emergency, not really—they were just being "thorough"—but that thoroughness cost me the first hour of being a mother. I still wonder if my baby felt that same cold, sterile distance I did. It hurts, even now, because I can’t get that time back.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I think I fell into the trap of believing that because I had "prepared" so hard—taking all the supplements, watching my intake, obsessing over the books—that I could sort of negotiate my way into a textbook recovery. I expected to be tired, obviously, but I imagined myself leaning into that "new mother" glow I’d seen in brochures. I thought I’d be able to manage the physical healing while my husband handled the logistics, and we’d just sort of settle into this new rhythm.

The reality was so much harsher. My body felt like it had been through a car accident, not just a labor. The physical exhaustion was bone-deep, and it wasn't just the healing from the delivery—it was the emotional tax of it all. Because we don't have family nearby and our support system is basically just us, the "recovery" wasn't a time of resting. It was a time of pure, frantic survival. 

I’d be sitting there trying to breastfeed, my body aching in ways I hadn’t anticipated, and I’d have to get up to check the transit schedules or deal with a grocery delivery, all while feeling like I was still bleeding out physically and emotionally. Nobody told me how much the isolation would physically hurt. You aren't just healing your body; you're trying to keep your head above water in a vacuum. It wasn't the slow, graceful transition I’d pictured. It was just another hurdle I had to leap over while being completely, utterly depleted.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

It’s the sheer, crushing vulnerability of it all. Everyone talks about the stitches or the exhaustion, but nobody tells you about the *hollowness* in your own skin after the baby is out. I expected to feel a sense of relief, like a weight lifted, but instead, I felt like my body was suddenly a foreign, broken instrument. 

The biggest surprise was how the lack of a support network made every single physical ache feel like a catastrophe. If I’m in pain, I can’t just rest; I have to keep standing up because there’s no one else to hold the baby, no one else to handle the mundane tasks that never stop. That constant, gnawing worry about whether I’m "doing it right"—especially with the added pressure of being an older first-time mom—felt like a physical weight on my chest, like I couldn't get a full breath.

I didn't expect the emotional toll to manifest so physically—the way the loneliness makes your heart physically race, or how the uncertainty of not having a mother or sister nearby to lean on makes your muscles stay permanently clenched. You’re expected to "bounce back," but how do you do that when your support system is just a couple of apps on your phone and a husband who is just as terrified as you are?

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I had romanticized the idea of breastfeeding, viewing it as the ultimate extension of the connection I’d been trying to build for nine months. I read the articles about it being "natural," as if that meant it would be intuitive and easy. I expected a gentle, quiet rhythm—that skin-to-skin contact that had been interrupted during the birth—where I’d finally feel like a mother.

The reality was a battlefield. My body, which I already felt disconnected from after the birth, just didn't seem to get the memo. The latch was agonizing, and every time I tried to feed, I felt that same sense of being "under-resourced" that I felt during my entire pregnancy. There was no village to turn to, no one to hand the baby to while I cried from the pain and the sheer, raw frustration. 

When I asked for help at the hospital, it felt like another clinical audit—they’d come in, watch for three minutes, give a technical tip, and disappear back into the fluorescent hallway. I didn't get support; I got instructions. And when those instructions didn't work, I felt like I was failing at the most basic level of motherhood. I wasn't just tired; I was completely hollowed out by the effort. Breastfeeding, which I thought would be my sanctuary, ended up being yet another source of anxiety.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

The gap wasn't just wide—it was an abyss. The support I was offered was purely functional. It was "here’s how you hold the baby, here’s how you check the latch, here’s the chart where you track the ounces." It was all efficiency, all data. It was as if they thought if they could just perfect the mechanics, the emotional burden would take care of itself.

But what I actually needed was someone to sit with me and tell me that it was okay if I was terrified. I needed someone to recognize that I was a forty-three-year-old mother who had just had the most intense, isolating experience of her life, and that my body was still reeling. I needed someone to say, "You are doing enough," instead of just pointing out that I needed to do *more* to get the baby to gain weight.

When the lactation consultant left the room, I’d be sitting there with my shirt open, feeling like a complete failure because the mechanics didn't feel natural and my heart wasn't in that "nurturing" place the brochures promised. I didn't need another tip on positioning; I needed a witness to my struggle. I needed to know that my isolation was valid and that I wasn't failing because I was "too old" or "not prepared." It was a clinical fix for an emotional, soul-deep exhaustion.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I remember reading those pamphlets in the clinic waiting room that warned about "baby blues" and "postpartum depression" as if they were just bullet points on a checklist. I expected a brief, manageable dip in mood, quickly corrected by the joy of having my baby here. I thought my history of loss would make me extra vigilant, and that being a mother would finally, magically, quiet that internal alarm bell that had been ringing for nine months.

The reality was a relentless, suffocating shadow. It wasn't just "the blues"—it was a deep, paralyzing grief mixed with sheer terror. Every time the baby cried, I didn't just hear a hunger cue; I heard a siren. My brain would immediately jump to the worst-case scenario. Because there was no family here to hand the baby to, no grandmother to say, "I've got her, you go shower," that constant, vibrating anxiety became my permanent state of being. 

It was like standing on a cliff’s edge. Every night felt like an endurance test. I felt this intense, crushing pressure to be the "perfect" mother to make up for my age and my previous loss, but I was so internally broken that I couldn't even feel the "joy" people kept talking about. It was just survival—hour by hour, day by day—in a silent house.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I thought that becoming a mother would be the final, missing piece of the puzzle—that it would give me a clear, settled identity. I expected to step into this role and feel like I’d finally "arrived" at the place I’d spent forty-three years working toward. I imagined the professional version of myself, the one who obsessed over charts and fetal health, would simply melt into this grounded, maternal version.

But that’s not what happened. Instead, I feel like I’ve been split in half. Part of me is still that woman who is desperately trying to be competent and informed, the one who’s constantly checking the weather or the bus schedules because the world feels so unpredictable. And the other part is this exhausted, raw, and deeply lonely mother who feels completely unmoored. 

I don’t feel like I’ve "arrived" anywhere; I feel like I’m hovering in a state of permanent transition. I’ve lost that sense of who I was before the pregnancy, but I haven't quite become who I want to be for my child. It’s like I’m constantly mourning the person I was while simultaneously struggling to keep the new one from drowning. It’s a strange, hollow kind of transformation—one that feels less like a bloom and more like a quiet, relentless breaking.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

It’s the loss of my intellectual autonomy that I struggle with most. Before the baby, my identity was built on being the person who did the research, the one who understood the mechanics, the one who wasn't just "the pregnant lady" but a capable person with a sharp mind. I really thought that once the baby was born, I would integrate those pieces—the "competent professional" and the "nurturing mother"—into one cohesive person.

But the sheer, relentless nature of the physical caretaking, compounded by our isolation, has eroded that professional, structured version of me. I don’t have the time or the mental bandwidth to be the "expert" on anything anymore. Every time I try to read a paper or keep up with my field, the baby cries, or I’m just too shattered to process the words. I feel like my brain has been scrubbed clean and replaced with nothing but anxiety and logistics.

I also miss the social side—not just having friends, but feeling like an adult in a world that isn't measured in diapers and breastfeeding sessions. I’m mourning the woman who could hold a conversation that didn't revolve around sleep schedules or developmental milestones. I didn't realize how much of my identity was anchored in being an active participant in a larger world, and now that my world has shrunk down to these four walls, I don't know where that woman went. I fear she’s just fading away.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I think I expected those check-ups to be a sort of "debrief"—a time for someone to look at me, the person who had survived the marathon of birth and the crushing weight of the first few weeks, and actually *ask* me how I was handling the debris. I expected a space where my physical recovery, the breastfeeding hurdles, and the absolute, paralyzing anxiety of being a first-time mother at forty-three would all be validated. I wanted a connection.

But the reality? It felt like another clinical audit. The focus was entirely on the baby’s weight gain or my own blood pressure—the same old checkboxes. They’d ask "How are you feeling?" and before I could even find the words to explain the hollowness or the panic, they were already moving on to the next task. It felt like I was being processed through a machine that had no space for the human element.

I wanted someone to say, "I see you’re struggling, you’re doing this without a safety net, let’s find you some help." Instead, I got a pat on the back and a generic "You’re doing great, mom." It felt so shallow. It was as if they thought if they ignored the emotional wreckage, it wasn't there. I walked out of those appointments feeling just as invisible as I did the day I walked into the hospital.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

I brought up the anxiety—the way the world feels like it’s constantly closing in on me, the way I wake up in the middle of the night terrified that I’ve missed a sign or that something is wrong. I tried to articulate that this wasn't just "baby blues," but something sharper, more like a physical weight that I couldn't shake. I wanted to talk about how the isolation—having no family, no village, no one to share the load—was making it impossible to feel "recovered."

They listened, but it was like they were hearing a report on a minor, expected symptom rather than a cry for help. They gave me a survey—one of those standard "on a scale of one to ten" depression screenings—and because I didn't hit the absolute "red flag" threshold for an immediate crisis, they basically shrugged. They told me, "It’s normal to feel overwhelmed as a new mom, especially at your age."

That word—*normal*—was like a slap in the face. It made me feel like my pain was just part of the price of admission. They didn't offer any resources for counseling or support groups; they didn't even ask if my husband and I had a plan for the mental toll. They just checked a box on their tablet and sent me out the door. It felt like they were managing their liability, not my health.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I’ll be honest—I had this fantasy that returning to work would be my salvation. I thought it would be the one place where I could be "Camellia" again—the competent, rational person who isn't defined by diaper changes or sleep-deprived crying. I expected to feel a sense of relief, a way to reclaim that part of my identity that felt so eroded. I figured I’d manage the logistics, find the right balance, and finally feel like a whole person again.

The reality was… jarring. It felt like being dropped into a high-stakes, high-speed game where I’d forgotten all the rules. I was trying to hold a professional, sharp exterior while my heart was literally somewhere else, tethered to the baby and vibrating with that same persistent, low-level anxiety about whether we were safe. Every meeting, every email, felt like an exercise in performance. I was faking "normal" because I didn't want my colleagues to see how much I was actually struggling.

There was no "reclaiming" my old self. There was just this deep, exhausting split. I was physically at my desk, but my mind was constantly scanning for the next crisis, the next bill, the next logistical failure. I felt like an imposter in my own career, and when I came home, I felt like an imposter in my own nursery. I didn't find balance; I just found a different, more expensive kind of exhaustion.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

We both went into this thinking we’d be this unstoppable team. We spent so much time talking about how we’d share the sleepless nights and the feeding, about how we’d prioritize our connection. I expected that even in the chaos, we’d be looking at each other across the nursery, sharing a look that said, "We’re doing this together."

But the reality has been closer to two people drowning while tethered to the same anchor. Because we have zero social support—no parents, no siblings, no friends nearby—every single decision, every middle-of-the-night panic, and every logistical nightmare falls entirely on us. There’s no break. We’re both so depleted that when we finally have a moment, we’re often just snapping at each other, not out of malice, but out of sheer, bone-deep exhaustion.

It’s hard to be a partner when you’re both in survival mode. The "team" aspect is there, but it’s become strictly operational. It’s "Did you check the transit schedule?" or "Did the baby eat?" instead of "How are you?" We’re managing a life, but we’ve lost the space to be a couple. We’re both so terrified of failing this child that we’ve stopped really seeing each other. It’s the most lonely I’ve ever felt, even when he’s right there next to me.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected maternity care to be the scaffold for my transformation into motherhood—a steady, knowing hand that would help me navigate the minefields of age and past trauma. I thought that by being educated and "compliant," I would be granted a kind of dignity in the process. I expected the system to be a partner that validated my fears and prioritized my humanity as much as my clinical outcomes.

Instead, the most significant gap—the gaping, painful hole—was the total absence of human context. The system treated me like a broken engine that needed to be kept running, ignoring the woman whose life was built on a foundation of anxiety and isolation. They didn’t see my history, they didn’t see my lack of a village, and they didn’t see the terror that defined my every waking hour. They gave me charts, monitors, and protocols, but they denied me the one thing I truly needed: a witness to my experience who didn't view me as a statistic. 

I’ve come out of this journey feeling like I was processed through a machine that couldn't care less about the person it was delivering. I have my baby, and I’m thankful for that, but I’m still mourning the version of me that deserved to be held, heard, and seen.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

I would shift the system’s primary objective from "managing risk" to "sustaining the mother." Right now, the entire infrastructure—from the clinics to the hospitals—is hyper-focused on the clinical markers: the blood pressure, the fetal heart rate, the weight gain, the centimeters of dilation. If you’re labeled "medium-risk" or "geriatric," that clinical gaze becomes even more intense, narrowing until the person underneath it is effectively erased.

If I could change one thing, it would be the integration of social and emotional support as a *non-negotiable* part of medical care. The system needs to recognize that a mother’s safety isn't just about her vitals; it’s about her capacity to cope, her access to transportation, and her network of support. 

I want a system that builds a "village" for you. If you’re isolated, if you don't have a car to get to the hospital, if you’re carrying the weight of a past loss—the system should be designed to bridge those gaps, not just ignore them. I want doctors and midwives who have the time to sit, listen, and coordinate care that includes social workers and mental health support as standard practice, not as an afterthought or a "referral" if you happen to fail a depression screening. We need to be treated as human beings, not just biological projects.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think what I want to add, more than anything, is just how much the "wait" defines you. Every day I spent waiting for the next scan, waiting for the lab results, waiting to see if I’d make it to term—it was like holding my breath for nine months. I never felt like I was allowed to just *be* pregnant. I was always "awaiting the outcome." 

And then, when I finally gave birth, I realized I’m still waiting. Now, I’m waiting for the next developmental milestone, waiting for my body to feel like my own again, waiting for the exhaustion to finally lift so I can actually experience the joy of being a mother instead of just the labor of it. 

I’m 43, and I feel like I’ve spent the most significant year of my life perpetually braced for impact. I think the system needs to understand that when you have a history of loss, you don't just "get over it" once the baby is here. The fear doesn't disappear; it just changes shape. I’m not asking for them to fix my life, but I am asking them to stop treating my anxiety like it’s an inconvenience to their efficiency. I’m a mother, and I’m still here, still navigating, and still waiting for someone to finally just... hear me. Thank you for listening today. It felt, for once, like I wasn't speaking into a void.
